content.text:
If I had to pick one recent book that feels genuinely new, it would be Kaveh Akbar’s *Martyr!* (2024). On the surface, it follows Cyrus Shams, an Iranian American poet in the shaky early days of sobriety, obsessed with death, sainthood, and what it might mean to live a life that matters. As he becomes drawn to a terminally ill artist whose work turns mortality into performance, the novel opens outward into something stranger and more searching. That premise could have produced a dour, over-intellectual book. Instead, Akbar writes something rare: a novel that is philosophically restless, emotionally raw, and unexpectedly funny.

What makes *Martyr!* stand out first is its voice. Akbar is a poet, and you can hear it in nearly every sentence—not in a showy, ornamental way, but in the precision of his language and the way he can make an abstract thought land like a confession. Cyrus is self-dramatizing, wounded, searching, and often hilarious; he is the kind of narrator who can veer from absurdity to heartbreak in a single paragraph. The title sounds severe, but the book keeps surprising you with tenderness and comic timing.

The novel is about addiction and grief, but also about immigration, inherited trauma, art, faith, and the seductive glamour of self-destruction. Akbar manages to hold all of that without turning the book into a thesis. Its real achievement is that it asks huge questions—about martyrdom, meaning, and whether suffering can ever be transformed into beauty—without becoming stiff or solemn. It feels alive to contradiction. It is serious but never humorless, intellectually ambitious but never bloodless.

A lot of recent novels are either impeccably crafted or emotionally urgent. *Martyr!* is both. It has the energy of a debut, the confidence of a seasoned writer, and the strange afterglow of a book that is trying to discover something genuinely difficult about being alive. That, more than anything, is what makes it stand out.

If I had to pick one, I’d say **_Invasion of the Body Snatchers_ (1978)**.

What makes it so interesting is that it shows what a remake can do **other than just retell the same story with newer effects**. - creepy without relying on jump scares

content.text:
To me, the most interesting statue is **Bernini’s *Apollo and Daphne***.

If you’ve never seen it, imagine walking up to what looks, at first, like a scene caught in the single most dramatic frame of a film. It is carved from white marble, life-sized but somehow more alive than life. Two figures are in motion: Apollo, the young god, is rushing forward, and Daphne, the woman he is chasing, is twisting away from him at the exact instant her body begins to turn into a tree.

That transformation is what makes it unforgettable.

Apollo’s body is all pursuit—one leg forward, torso lifted, one hand just reaching her. He doesn’t look calm or monumental in the way many statues do; he looks breathless, intent, almost certain he is about to catch her. Daphne is the opposite: she is fleeing, but in the same moment she is also slipping out of the human world. Her toes seem to root into the ground. Bark climbs her legs. Her fingers stretch upward and become sprays of leaves. Her hair loosens into branches. Her face is still human enough to register fear and astonishment, but the rest of her is already becoming something else.

What is astonishing is that all of this is marble.

Marble usually makes you think of weight, stillness, coldness. Bernini makes it feel light, fragile, and unstable. The leaves look as if they could tremble in a breeze. The skin seems soft. The drapery whips around the bodies like cloth caught mid-turn. It has that rare quality some sculptures have where your mind keeps insisting it must be moving, even though it is perfectly still.

The effect on the viewer is complicated, which is part of why I find it so interesting. First there is sheer admiration: “How can stone do this?” Then there is the emotional reaction. From a distance it can seem beautiful and graceful, almost theatrical. But the longer you look, the stranger and sadder it becomes. You realize this isn’t just a transformation; it’s an escape through self-erasure. Daphne avoids being possessed by ceasing to remain herself. So the statue is both dazzling and disturbing. It gives you wonder and discomfort at the same time.

What I love most is that it rewards walking around it. From one angle, it looks like a chase. From another, it looks like a miracle. From another, almost like a horror scene. It changes as you move, which makes it feel less like an object and more like an event.

A lot of statues feel like they were made to be admired. *Apollo and Daphne* feels like it was made to make you stop breathing for a second. It shows not just a body, but a moment when one reality becomes another. That, to me, is what makes it the most interesting statue I know.

If I had to pick one, I’d choose **Duff’s device**.

It’s a famous C hack that looks like nonsense the first time you see it, but it’s completely legal C and genuinely clever.

```c
void duff_copy(char *to, const char *from, int count) {
    if (count <= 0) return;

    int n = (count + 7) / 8;

    switch (count % 8) {
        case 0: do { *to++ = *from++;
        case 7:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 6:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 5:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 4:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 3:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 2:      *to++ = *from++;
        case 1:      *to++ = *from++;
                } while (--n > 0);
    }
}
```

## Why this looks wrong

At a glance, it seems like it **should not compile** because:

- the `case` labels are inside a `do ... while` loop
- the `switch` appears to jump into the **middle of a loop body**
- there are no `break`s, so execution just falls through everything

It feels like a parser exploit.

## Why it actually works

Duff’s device works because of three C rules:

1. **A `switch` is basically a jump to a label.**
   It evaluates `count % 8` and jumps to the matching `case`.

2. **`case` labels are just labels on statements.**
   They don’t have to sit neatly at the top level of a block. In C, they can label statements inside the switch’s controlled statement, even inside a loop body.

3. **A `do ... while` loop executes its body first.**
   So once the `switch` jumps into the middle of that body, execution continues normally until the end of the loop, then the loop condition runs.

## What it’s doing

The point is to copy `count` bytes, but faster than a simple one-byte-at-a-time loop.

It manually **unrolls** the loop by 8 copies at a time.  
The awkward part of loop unrolling is handling the leftover 1–7 bytes when `count` isn’t a multiple of 8.

Duff’s device solves both problems at once:

- `count % 8` decides where to jump into the unrolled body
- the first pass copies only the leftover bytes
- later passes copy full chunks of 8

## Example: `count = 10`

Then:

- `n = (10 + 7) / 8 = 2`
- `count % 8 = 2`

So execution jumps to `case 2`.

That means the first pass does only:

```c
*to++ = *from++;   // case 2
*to++ = *from++;   // case 1
```

So 2 bytes are copied.

Then the `while (--n > 0)` runs:

- `n` goes from 2 to 1
- condition is true, so the loop repeats

Now the loop starts from the top of the `do` block, so it executes all 8 copy statements.

Total copied:

- first pass: 2 bytes
- second pass: 8 bytes
- total: 10 bytes

That’s the whole trick.

## Why I think it’s so interesting

Because it’s one of those rare hacks that is:

- surprising
- ugly
- clever
- and still **actually correct**

A lot of famous “hacks” are really just undefined behavior or language abuse.  
Duff’s device is different: it looks impossible, but it works because it uses the language rules *exactly as written*.

It also teaches a deep lesson:

> `switch` is not a fancy `if/else`.  
> It’s closer to a structured jump table with fallthrough.

Once you understand that, the code stops looking magical and starts looking like a very compressed control-flow trick.

## Would I use it today?

Usually, no.

Modern compilers are very good at loop optimization, and this is hard to read and maintain. In most real code, a normal loop is better.

But as a programming hack, it’s fantastic because it feels illegal while being perfectly valid.

If you want, I can also show:
- a **step-by-step visual trace** of Duff’s device,
- a **clean equivalent version** written normally,
- or another favorite hack, like a **quine** or the **XOR swap trick**.

content.text:
There isn’t an objectively “most interesting” kind of proof, but my favorite is **proof by contradiction**.

Why? Because it has a dramatic shape:

1. Assume the statement you want is **false**.
2. Follow that assumption carefully.
3. Show it leads to something impossible.
4. Conclude the assumption was wrong, so the original statement must be **true**.

It feels a bit like solving a mystery by showing the “other story” can’t possibly fit the evidence.

### Simple example: There are infinitely many prime numbers

Let’s prove this by contradiction.

#### Claim
There are infinitely many primes.

#### Proof
Assume the opposite: suppose there are only **finitely many** primes.

So we could list them all:

\[
p_1, p_2, p_3, \dots, p_n
\]

Now build a new number:

\[
N = p_1p_2p_3\cdots p_n + 1
\]

This number \(N\) is one more than the product of all the primes on our list.

Now ask: can any listed prime divide \(N\)?

- If you divide \(N\) by \(p_1\), the remainder is 1.
- Same for \(p_2\), \(p_3\), and so on.
- So **none** of the primes on the list divides \(N\).

But every integer greater than 1 is either:
- prime itself, or
- divisible by some prime.

So \(N\) must either be prime, or have a prime factor.

Either way, that prime is **not on our list**.

That contradicts the assumption that we had already listed **all** primes.

So our assumption must be false.

Therefore, **there are infinitely many prime numbers**.

---

I think this kind of proof is especially beautiful because you don’t prove the statement directly—you show that the alternative collapses under its own weight. If you want, I can also show a simple example of **mathematical induction**, which is another very elegant proof style.

To me, the most interesting obsolete profession is the **resurrectionist**—the body-snatcher who supplied cadavers to anatomy schools before legal donation made the trade unnecessary. It fascinates me because it existed in a moral contradiction: society wanted skilled surgeons, but recoiled from giving them bodies to learn on. So a shadow profession grew up between graveyard and lecture hall.

Here’s a day-in-the-life sketch of one.

**A Resurrection Man, London, 1828**

I wake at noon, because men in my line keep the wrong hours for honest labor.

The room is gray with winter light. Dirt from last night is still worked into the cracks of my hands, and no amount of scrubbing ever quite sends churchyard clay away. My wife has left bread on the table. She asks whether there will be coal tomorrow. I say yes.

That is usually the first lie of the day: not that there will be money, but that it comes clean.

By afternoon I am in a taproom near the market, where undertakers’ boys, sextons, and gravediggers drink small ale and talk too freely for a coin. Priests know the names of the dead, but these men know where they lie, who watches them, and how recently the soil was turned.

A seamstress was buried yesterday in the parish ground off Clerkenwell. Poor family. They spent what they had on the coffin and candles; there will be no iron cage over the grave, no hired men to sit up all night beyond the first burst of grief. I buy the boy another drink and say nothing more.

The rest of the day is waiting.

I mend a strap, oil the hinge of my lantern, fold a square of sacking, and sit by the window pretending to be a porter or carter or any other man whose back aches for respectable reasons. Neighbors nod to me, but never warmly. A man can carry the smell of his trade even when no one can name it.

At midnight my partner comes.

The city has shut its eyes. Rain slicks the lanes and keeps decent people indoors. We walk without hurry; hurry is more suspicious than darkness. At the churchyard wall we stop and listen until even the watchman’s cough has moved to the far side of the ground.

Then we go over.

The work itself is hard, wet, and uglier than the tales make it. Mud sucks at the boots. Cold gets into the wrists. Wood gives with a damp groan. I do not look long at faces. Faces make claims. We work by touch as much as sight, quickly and quietly, each movement practiced because fear has rehearsed it a hundred times. The law, in its wisdom, is gentler about a stolen corpse than a stolen shroud, so the linen stays behind. Even in crime there are technicalities.

When we lift her free, she is lighter than I expected. They almost always are.

We settle her into the sack and make the ground look as though sorrow alone disturbed it. Then we take the narrow streets toward the anatomy rooms, two laboring men with a burden no one wishes to inspect too closely. Dawn is only a paling at the edges of the sky when the porter opens the back door.

Inside, the place smells of spirits, soap, and iron. A surgeon in shirtsleeves pulls back the sack just far enough to judge freshness. He never meets my eye for long. Men like him require my trade and despise it at the same moment. He pays in guineas, counted fast. Upstairs I can hear benches scraping and young voices rising: students arriving for the morning lecture.

By breakfast, the seamstress will no longer be a daughter, or a parish burden, or a woman whose hands went raw with needlework. She will be *the subject*. An arm will be lifted. A muscle named. A nerve traced cleanly by a boy who may one day save a living patient because he first learned the map on her.

That is the bargain beneath the horror.

Better, I tell myself, a surgeon learn on the dead than butcher the living.

I buy coal on the way home. Bread too. The market is opening now, and respectable people are beginning their day as mine ends. Bells ring for morning prayer. A cart rattles past with fresh vegetables; somewhere else, another funeral will be setting out.

When I lie down at last, there are a few coins under the mattress and black earth still caught in my nails. If my neighbors knew exactly how the money was earned, they would cross themselves at my door. Yet if one of them were ever laid on an operating table, pale with fear, they would pray for a surgeon with steady hands.

And steady hands, in my time, are taught by men like me.

If you want, I can also do one for a **sin-eater**, **knocker-up**, or **gong farmer**—all excellent obsolete professions in very different ways.

If I had to pick one, I’d say **hot pot** is the most interesting meal.

Why? Because it’s not just a plate of food—it’s a **process**. Most meals arrive finished. Hot pot keeps changing while you eat it. The broth deepens, the flavors layer, and the meal sort of tells a story from start to finish.

A few things make it especially interesting:

- **It evolves.** The first few minutes taste different from the last. By the end, the broth has become a record of everything that went into it.
- **It balances control and surprise.** You can plan what you want, but the meal still changes in ways you didn’t fully predict.

I also think it’s interesting because it says a lot about people. You can learn a weird amount about someone from how they do hot pot: patient or impulsive, adventurous or cautious, organized or chaotic, sauce minimalist or sauce engineer.

content.text:
Setting aside the obvious answer of the Sun, I’d pick **Eta Carinae**—more precisely, the **Eta Car system**.

If I were defending that choice to another astronomer, my argument would be this: Eta Car is not just peculiar; it has extraordinary **astrophysical leverage**. It sits at the intersection of multiple unsolved problems in stellar physics, and unlike many extreme objects, it is close and bright enough that we can actually study those problems in detail.

First, it is one of the best laboratories we have for the **upper mass/luminosity limit of stars**. The primary is a near-Eddington, extremely massive star whose evolution is dominated by mass loss, but the relevant mass-loss physics is still not well understood. Line-driven winds are only part of the story; Eta Car forces us to confront **violent, eruptive mass loss**, asphericity, and the possibility that binary interaction is central to how the most massive stars shed their envelopes.

Second, the **Great Eruption** makes it almost uniquely valuable. We very rarely get a giant, non-terminal stellar outburst observed historically and then preserved as resolved ejecta. Eta Car survived while ejecting on the order of **10 solar masses** and producing the **Homunculus Nebula**, which is essentially a fossil record of the event: geometry, kinematics, dust formation, momentum budget, and chemical structure are all encoded there. With **light echoes**, we can even recover spectroscopic information about the nineteenth-century eruption from different viewing angles. That is an astonishingly rich data set for something that happened before modern astrophysics existed.

Third, the system is also a **highly eccentric colliding-wind binary** with a 5.54-year cycle. That periodicity turns the source into a repeatable experiment in shock physics, wind-wind interaction, radiative inhibition/braking, ionization changes, and particle acceleration. Eta Car is useful across the spectrum: radio, IR, optical, UV, X-ray, and even gamma rays. Very few stellar objects are simultaneously that extreme and that diagnostically accessible.

Fourth, it matters well beyond the object itself. Eta Car is the nearest high-information analog for phenomena we increasingly infer in unresolved extragalactic data: **SN impostors, pre-supernova outbursts, dense asymmetric circumstellar media, and eruptive mass loss before core collapse**. Understanding Eta Car directly improves how we interpret Type IIn progenitors and other interacting transients. In that sense, it is not just an odd Galactic celebrity; it is a **Rosetta stone** for a whole class of massive-star behavior.

And finally, it evolves on **humanly interesting timescales**. Most stellar astrophysics is reconstructed statistically from populations. Eta Car, by contrast, has changed dramatically within the historical record, and it continues to show secular evolution. That alone makes it rare.

So my short version would be: **Eta Carinae is the most interesting star because it compresses an unreasonable fraction of frontier stellar astrophysics into one nearby, observable system**. It is simultaneously a problem in stellar structure, radiative hydrodynamics, binary interaction, circumstellar-medium formation, and transient astrophysics.

If you wanted the most scientifically foundational answer, I’d still say the **Sun**. But if the criterion is “which single non-solar star gives us the richest mix of extreme physics, open questions, and observational traction?”, I think Eta Carinae is the strongest case.

For me, it’s **Frankenstein’s creature**—not just because he’s iconic, but because he’s tragic, intelligent, eloquent, and morally torn in a way that still feels modern. Here’s a short original addition featuring him, set after the end of Mary Shelley’s novel.

**Mercy**

When I left the vessel that bore my creator’s corpse, I meant to keep my oath: to build a pyre upon the northern ice and let fire finish the work that nature had begun. For two days I traveled over groaning floes. On the third, through driven snow, I saw a hut of turf and timber beside a black inlet. I turned toward it only for fuel.

Inside, an old man sat by a dead hearth, his sightless eyes fixed on nothing. At his feet lay a little girl wrapped in skins, breathing in feverish catches. At my step the old man lifted his head.

“Runa?” he said. “Have you come back?”

I had not heard a human voice address me without terror since the blind De Lacey called me friend. “No,” I answered. “A stranger.”

“Then God has sent one,” he said. “The storm took our boat. The wood is wet, and my granddaughter burns.”

He did not ask what I was. He heard only a voice.

I went back into the dark, tore driftwood from the shore, and broke it against the stones. Soon the hearth was burning. I found a cask of fish buried in snow, mended the roof with a rag of sailcloth, and warmed broth with hands made more fit for murder than mercy. Several times the child stirred. Once she looked full into my face. I expected a cry. Instead she whispered, “You are cold,” and laid her hand against mine before sleep reclaimed her.

Toward morning the old man ate and said, “I have no gift for you, friend. Not even your name.”

“I have none,” I replied.

He was silent. Then he said, “All men have a name in another’s need. Yours tonight is Mercy.”

Never had my creator bestowed on me a thing so generous.

At dawn three fishermen entered. One saw me at the hearth and cried out as if Hell itself had risen from the embers. A harpoon grazed my shoulder and struck the beam behind me. The child woke screaming, not at me, but at them.

“No!” she cried. “He made the fire!”

I could have crushed the weapon and its master. Instead I cast it aside and passed through them into the snow. Their terror pursued me farther than their courage.

From the shore I looked back once. The old man had come to the doorway, one hand on the child’s shoulder, turning his sightless face to the wind. “Mercy,” he called, “may Heaven keep you.”

I resumed my journey northward. I did not repent my crimes; they walked beside me still. Yet for the first time I understood that though I had been denied fellowship, I had not been denied the power to confer it. I went on toward my fire carrying, amid all my memories of hatred, one frail and human blessing.

My vote goes to the **diving bell spider**, *Argyroneta aquatica*.

If I were writing it for a natural history museum exhibit, it would read something like this:

---

## Diving Bell Spider  
*Argyroneta aquatica*  
**The spider that carried air underwater**

Most spiders are creatures of dry land. The diving bell spider is the astonishing exception. Hidden among pondweed in quiet freshwater pools and marshes across Europe and parts of Asia, this small brown arachnid spends nearly its entire life beneath the surface.

Its body is built for an unusual existence. Fine, water-repellent hairs trap a silvery film of air around the abdomen and legs whenever the spider dives. Underwater, it gleams like a drop of mercury. That shining coat is not just beautiful—it is part of the spider’s life-support system.

The diving bell spider spins silk among submerged plants, creating a small dome-shaped chamber. Then it swims to the surface, collects a bubble of air against its body, and carries that bubble down to the silk retreat. Trip after trip, it inflates the chamber until it becomes a true underwater shelter: a **diving bell**.

Inside this bubble-filled room, the spider breathes, rests, molts, feeds, mates, and shelters its young. The bell functions as more than a simple air pocket. Oxygen from the surrounding water diffuses into it, allowing the spider to remain submerged for long periods, though the bell must still be topped up from time to time.

From this underwater home, the spider hunts. Sensitive silk threads and the movements of the water alert it to passing prey. It rushes out to seize small aquatic animals—especially insect larvae and tiny crustaceans—using venom to subdue them. Often it drags its catch back into the bell, where it can feed in air rather than open water.

Its family life is just as unusual. In most spider species, females are larger than males. In the diving bell spider, the male is often the larger and more wide-ranging sex. He searches the underwater vegetation for a female’s bell, and courtship takes place in this submerged world of silk and air. The female later builds a protected nursery chamber, lays her eggs, and guards the young after they hatch.

The diving bell spider is one of evolution’s most remarkable experiments: a spider that did not merely visit the water, but made a home there. Its survival depends on clean, well-vegetated wetlands. Where ponds are drained or polluted, this extraordinary underwater hunter disappears.

### At a Glance
- **Group:** Arachnid, order Araneae  
- **Habitat:** Still or slow-moving freshwater with abundant plants  
- **Diet:** Aquatic insects, larvae, and small crustaceans  
- **Signature adaptation:** An air-filled silk “diving bell” used as a lung, shelter, and nursery  

---

If you want, I can also do the same kind of exhibit text for a different arachnid—like a **peacock spider**, **tailless whip scorpion**, or **emperor scorpion**.

For me, the most interesting emotion is **anticipation**—it’s where hope and fear share the same heartbeat.

> I live in the pause before the phone rings, in the held breath before the envelope is opened, in the thin bright wire between what is and what might be. I am never still. I pace. I rehearse. I build futures out of a glance, a silence, a delayed reply. Give me a closed door and I will paint ten lives behind it.  
>  
> I make your coffee colder because you forget to drink it. I turn clocks cruel. I can make a minute feel like a hallway with no end. But I am not only dread. Sometimes I am the trembling sweetness before reunion: the train approaching, the footsteps on the stairs, the first note before the curtain rises. I am the spark that keeps your eyes on the horizon.  
>  
> People mistake me for impatience, but impatience wants to escape the waiting; I want to inhabit it, to fill it until it hums. I sharpen colors. I turn small omens luminous: headlights, footsteps, the hush before dawn. I teach your body to listen. Even your fear, in my hands, becomes a kind of devotion: proof that something matters enough to shake you.  
>  
> And when the moment finally arrives—when the call comes, the result is revealed, the face appears in the doorway—I vanish at once. That is my strangeness, my beauty. I am built entirely from maybe. I exist to escort you to the threshold, then disappear the instant you cross it, leaving your pulse to explain where I have been.
